notFoundDesc: No catalog entry for “{{slug}}”. Try browsing the disease list.
heroLedePart1: Right after a diagnosis the hardest part isn’t the missing answers — it’s
heroLedeBold1: not knowing what to even ask.
heroLedePart2: That you have to see the diagnosis through yourself. That a doctor who knows
heroLedePart3: disease exists. That guidelines, foundations, and trials exist. Below you’re not searching —
heroLedeBold2: you’re reading a map of the things nobody told you about.
privacyBodyPart1: You can load it privately — facts are extracted locally in your browser, the original never reaches us. Then the map below adapts to
introTitle: Six things you don’t know to ask about — in the order you’ll need them.
introSub: This isn’t a feature list. It’s the path a family walks after a diagnosis — each stop tells you what exists and what to do with it.
step1Hook: Nobody tells you the diagnosis is yours to double-check
step1Title: First, make sure it’s really this disease — and this subtype.
step1LedeFdPart3: by biopsy alone. The guideline says outright when a biopsy
step1LedeFdPart4: , yet it is sometimes done routinely. Before anyone proposes treatment, the diagnosis and subtype (mono- vs polyostotic) must be certain.
step1LedeGenericPart1: Many rare diseases look like more common ones on a first pass. Before anyone proposes treatment, the diagnosis and subtype must be confirmed — often
step1LedeGenericPart2: and on imaging, not by one test in isolation.
step1AnchorQuote: “In Warsaw our son had a histopathology the guideline didn’t require — and got a wrong diagnosis. Only the CT in Olsztyn and the GNAS test settled it.”
step1CtaConfirm: What to confirm, and in what order
step2Hook: Not every surgeon or endocrinologist knows this disease
step2Title: A handful of doctors truly know {{name}}. Find them.
step2LedePart1: In a rare disease the most valuable — and hardest to find — person is a doctor who
step2LedePart2: knows this condition. We show the
step2LedePart3: (from PubMed: whether they have published on it, co-authored studies, or led them) and family recommendations — sorted by distance from you.
step2AnchorQuote: “We found the best doctors by word of mouth and by chance at a check-up — not from a search engine. That’s why a parent can add a doctor here, with a note.”
step3HookExists: Yes — guidance for this disease already exists
step3HookAssembling: Guidance is being assembled for this disease
step3TitleExists: Someone already wrote down how to manage {{name}}. Read it before your visit.
step3TitleAssembling: Read what's known about managing {{name}} before your visit.
step3LedePart1: From the guidelines we pull out
step3LedePart2: — diagnosis, monitoring, red flags, when to seek a second opinion — instead of a wall of text written for a specialist. Every sentence links to its source, and newer “to consider” recommendations are clearly labelled.
step3AnchorQuotePart1: “The FD guideline said plainly what is
step3AnchorQuotePart2: done in a child — and Munich recommended exactly that. The knowledge existed; it just hadn’t reached us.”
step3CtaSeeFar: See what we have so far
step4Hook: Travel to specialists and tests cost money — help exists
step4Title: You don’t have to fund this journey alone.
step4LedePart2: . We also show how to start and spread a fundraiser for travel to specialists, visits, and tests — something a freshly-diagnosed family rarely thinks of at the start.
step4AnchorQuote: “The international FDMAS Alliance had been there all along — but we had to find it ourselves, over months.”
step5Hook: Taking part in a trial can be the best available therapy
step5Title: Check whether someone is testing something for your child.
step5LedePart1: Actively recruiting trials, linked to ClinicalTrials.gov, sorted by distance. When you load your case privately,
step5LedeBold: we’ll alert you when a trial matching your mutation appears
step5LedePart2: — so you don’t have to check by hand every week.
step0Hook: And if no one knows yet what’s wrong?
step0PredxLedePart1: Sometimes the problem isn’t “I have a diagnosis, what now” but
step0PredxLedePart2: . The interview works iteratively: it takes a broad first pass, then — depending on your answers — can search the literature before asking the next question. The goal is not diagnostic: it’s
step0PredxLedePart3: — which leads to explore, which specialist to consider, what to ask — written up as a document to discuss with your doctor.
safetyBold: Everything here is a starting point for a conversation with your doctor — not a recommendation.
safetyBody: We show official guidelines plainly; “to consider” recommendations and AI drafts always carry a label, a rationale, and a “discuss with your doctor” note. Your doctor makes the decisions — we help you know what to ask.
footerPart1: — open infrastructure for rare-disease guidelines, maintained by the GeneQuest Foundation. We test every design decision with one question:
footerEm: would this have helped our son?
